title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several key items including a resolution supporting state transportation funding, awarded the recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel for $126,900, and directed staff to create enforcement language for sewer discharge violations with fines up to $100/day. The committee also approved construction service proposals for upcoming road projects and acknowledged receiving over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years.

agenda.item: Call to order
Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Public comment
Approval of April 13, 2026 meeting minutes
Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
CIP update
Street operation update
Utility operating update
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC annual report
Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Recycled asphalt crushing contract award
Sanitary sewer discharge violation discussion
Water and sewer connection agreement for Radkkey Avenue duplex
Military Road construction agreement
Construction service proposals for Alderson/Jelnik and Bladel projects

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project cost. This 2029 project will likely be moved up to 2028. Michael noted the village has secured over $9 million in grant funding in the past 4-5 years. Updates were also provided on Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating, Schofield Avenue repairs by HOS Construction, and Fuller Street work by Melvin. A special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue was scheduled for May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM.

Staff reported positive feedback on the Kennedy Park Farmers Market's first weekend with the new parking arrangement. No complaints were received about traffic flow or parking despite concurrent baseball tournaments. A vendor interviewed said it was great, and an additional handicap spot was added from the prior week.

Staff reported road limits were removed on April 29th. Crews conducted mailbox and sign repairs, dewatering operations near Weston Avenue tributary for about a week, spring curb sweeping, hot mix patching, storm damage cleanup, and inlet repairs on Birch Street. Culverts failed on Jones Street requiring replacement with three 18-inch culverts. Equipment maintenance included removing plow equipment, applying anti-rust treatment, and servicing mowers and tractors.

Josh reported significant clear water infiltration into the sanitary sewer system, with April showing 37 million gallons of water pumped into the system but 53-54 million gallons treated - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water. An INI study is planned to identify problem areas. Staff caught one residence on Kathleen where sump pump discharge was entering the sanitary system. Water main flushing notices were being mailed with flushing to begin the following week.

Michael presented the annual report showing net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss is at 6%. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. The utility serves approximately 6,100 customers - 5,250 in Weston, 830 in Rothschild, and 5 in other areas. Cash reserves stand at $6.8 million. Acknowledged unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution requests long-term sustainable transportation funding from the state. He noted that while the village receives grants for capital projects, recurring operational costs for street maintenance and snow plowing are subsidized with general tax dollars. Wisconsin has relatively affordable vehicle ownership costs compared to neighboring states. Approved unanimously to recommend to village board.

King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for crushing approximately 30,000 tons of recycled material at the Ryan Street site, totaling $126,900. Michael demonstrated the program saves approximately $11/cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, translating to about $130,000 in savings across this year's three projects. Approved unanimously.

Staff sought guidance on enforcement of sewer ordinance violations for clear water discharge. The committee discussed distinguishing between unknowing violations (first offense with notice to correct) versus willful violations. After discussion about using the upcoming INI study to identify violators, the committee directed staff to create enforcement language with fines starting at $100/day, consistent with the existing ordinance range of $100-$1,000/day. Motion passed unanimously.

A builder wants to construct a duplex on lot 1 at 2230 Radkkey Avenue. Since water/sewer mains don't extend to the lot, staff proposed allowing a temporary long lateral connection until Radkkey Avenue reconstruction. Committee added language requiring the main extension and assessment payment no later than end of 2029 construction season. Approved unanimously.

The committee approved an intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for Military Road construction from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Weston's share for water and sewer work is approximately $94,860 (sanitary sewer $6,156; water main $88,704). Steam Construction is the contractor. Water main must be relocated due to storm sewer conflicts. Approved unanimously.

For Alderson/Jelnik roundabout project, MSA was recommended at $37,315 (vs. Clark Dietz at $48,910). For Bladel Avenue project, Clark Dietz was recommended at $73,730 (vs. MSA at $70,400) because MSA proposed using a summer intern while Clark Dietz offered full-time experienced staff. Both recommendations approved unanimously.

actionItems: Staff to finalize May 28, 2026 special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue at 6:00 PM
Award recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton ($126,900 estimated total)
Staff to create enforcement language for sewer discharge violations with $100/day fines
Village board to approve Radkkey Avenue connection agreement with assessment deadline no later than 2029
Village board to approve Rothschild agreement for Military Road at estimated $94,860 village cost
Approve MSA for Alderson/Jelnik construction services ($37,315) and Clark Dietz for Bladel construction services ($73,730)
Resolution 2026-014 to go to village board for adoption
Norcon to begin Schofield Avenue concrete repairs Wednesday/Thursday
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water infiltration impact for future meeting
Staff to provide street lighting update at future meeting

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its first meeting after reorganization, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission noted that a competing balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, making Taste and Glow the sole hot air balloon event in the area.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Election of officers
Public comments
Minutes of the previous meeting - April 21st
Work product transmittals, educational presentations and reports
Old business
New business - Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff
Remarks from commission members
Adjournment

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated and elected as Chair unanimously. Cushing was nominated and elected as Vice Chair, with Jackson providing the second. Renee was nominated and elected as Secretary. All elections passed unanimously with no opposition.
Minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. Motion was seconded by Renee and passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill of 3308 Green Pastures Lane presented on behalf of Taste and Glow Balloonfest. He reported last year's attendance at approximately 11,000-12,000 people and announced that the competing Marshfield balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making them the sole hot air balloon event in the area. Commissioner Hodal questioned room tracking, noting the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year. The commission approved a $5,000 grant matching last year's amount, with Jackson seconding the motion. The motion passed unanimously. The commission requested better hotel room tracking data for future applications.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates, noting that Anastasia won the competition. The candidates toured Marathon County locations including Mullen's Cheese, and Renee noted this was the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County since the 1970s. The candidates expressed appreciation for the Marathon County community.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest for their annual event
Taste and Glow Balloonfest to provide hotel room usage data in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM (tentative)

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with all present commissioners voting in favor.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Open public comment period for items not on agenda
Disclosures and recusals
Approval of April 13, 2026 meeting minutes
Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
April 2026 building permits
Planning and development department monthly project update report
Commissioner remarks
Future agenda items and staff updates
Adjournment

discussions.item: Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive, noting it meets all B2 zoning requirements. Chief Jeremy explained the building was rotated to address challenges with a gas pipeline easement running through the property, which reduced fill costs significantly. The building will accommodate 50-60 officers for future growth beyond the current 40 officers. Discussion covered the alternate storage shed placement, landscaping compliance with current code, and potential future expansion to the north. A motion to recommend approval to the board of trustees with conditions 2 through 5 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Staff proposed a twice-annual inspection program for salt storage facilities within wellhead protection areas - one warm weather inspection focused on structural integrity and drainage, and one winter inspection focused on operational practices. A standardized inspection worksheet based on DOT practices was developed. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and should be contacted. Motion to approve the program with reporting back to both the Plan Commission and Public Works Committee passed unanimously.
Motion to approve the April 13, 2026 meeting minutes as presented passed unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
discussions.body: Approved unanimously without discussion.
Approved unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: Planning and development department monthly project update
discussions.body: Staff announced the first joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Staff will send calendar invites and emails to commissioners.

publicComment: Jim Pensson of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via online connection. He urged the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project, asking that taxpayers not be burdened with excessive landscaping costs similar to the Green Heck Turner building situation. He also requested that the salt shed inspection program include annual reporting on inspections conducted and results, noting some zoning complaints have gone unresolved for years.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive recommended for approval to Board of Trustees with conditions 2-5
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted with twice-annual inspections and reporting to Plan Commission and Public Works Committee
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility near Sternberg that has not gone through wellhead protection conditional use process
Staff to report back on salt shed inspection program findings in approximately one year
Joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee meeting for comprehensive plan scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Electric vehicle charging station regulations to be discussed at future meeting in the fall
Next regular meeting scheduled for Monday, June 8th, 2026 at 6:00 p.m.

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an orientation meeting for new board members, featuring educational presentations from department heads on the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, and Aging & Disability Resource Center. No action items were voted on besides approval of April 1st meeting minutes. The committee learned about departmental missions, budgets, and ongoing initiatives including homelessness response efforts and veteran suicide prevention programs.

agenda.item: Call to order and remote attendance confirmation
Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute - overview for new members
Review of standing committee ordinance section 2.04, missions and responsibilities
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veteran Service Office presentation by VSO Aaron Galindo
Aging & Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray
Next meeting date and adjournment

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavalli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. Motion carried unanimously with voice vote.

Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee structure under section 2.04 subsection 5, covering jurisdiction over Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, and affiliated entities including North Central Health Care and ADRC. He outlined reporting relationships with statutory boards including Social Services Board, Veteran Service Commission, and Board of Health. Chair Boots noted the strategic plan process will be a focus for the committee over the next two years.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented on the department's mission to prevent disease, promote health, and protect from environmental hazards. The department has 37.6 FTE staff across environmental health, nursing, community health improvement, and administration teams. She highlighted public health vending machines offering free resources including tick kits, the Nurse Family Partnership program with first graduates, and a new homelessness system response position. In response to Supervisor Kremes' question about pandemic preparedness, Suginaka confirmed staff are trained in emergency response with plans for measles and other infectious disease outbreaks.

Administrator Leonard provided overview of the county's second largest department with nearly 140 FTE, covering child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. He highlighted the successful Family Keys pilot program addressing housing instability, which was modified based on board feedback. The department's 2026 budget shows $22 million total revenue with approximately $7 million in tax levy.

VSO Aaron Galindo reported his department increased to 3.0 FTE in 2025 and customer interactions jumped from 5,900 to 8,149. The office facilitated $44 million annually in benefits to county veterans, with $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Key initiatives include partnership with Center for Veterans Issues for housing stability, surviving spouse benefits outreach, and suicide prevention efforts (Marathon County had 30 suicides in 2025, 7 were veterans). In response to Supervisor Kremes' questions about staffing adequacy, Galindo acknowledged the office is busy but managing with the new FTE position.

Executive Director Mike Ray presented on the four-county Aging & Disability Resource Center serving Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood counties. The agency provides information and assistance, meals on wheels, caregiver support, dementia care, and falls prevention. He noted significant financial challenges with nutrition program costs increasing 40-45% over four years while federal funding remained flat. The agency has approximately 60 FTE staff and relies on 385-400 volunteers. The levy remained flat from 2011 until requesting an increase in 2024.
Vice Chair Rosenberg caught an error in the announced next meeting date. The correct date was confirmed as Wednesday, June 3rd, not May 29th as initially stated.

actionItems: April 1st committee meeting minutes approved
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for separate full presentation before the committee
ADRC Executive Director to present to full board with 2025 annual report data
Strategic plan discussion to be added to upcoming agendas
Next meeting scheduled for Wednesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed ordinances on mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and discussed ongoing challenges with relocating the farmers market to Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and approved multiple license renewals for the upcoming licensing term.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, roll call, and Pledge of Allegiance
Public comments - Jim Pitel regarding engineering consultants and committee appointments
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fee amendments
Approval of previous meeting minutes
Committee and commission reports and chair updates
Department reports (Administrative, Clerk, Finance, Police, Parks, Planning, Public Works, Technology)
Consent agenda including vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances on mobile food vendors, committees, nuisances, and e-bikes
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Park and Recreation Impact Fee Amendment
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed increases to park impact fees from $300/$250 to $761 for single family, $563 for duplex, and $495 for multifamily units, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke during public comment questioning whether the village has real data on park maintenance costs. Trustee Clark supported the fee as justified given population growth. Ordinance 26-010 was approved unanimously, and Resolution 2026-015 amending the fee schedule was also approved unanimously.

Chief Finkey explained this ordinance allows mutual recognition of fire safety inspections among Safer, Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, Clover, and Riverside communities. A food truck inspected and stickered by one department would not need reinspection in other participating municipalities. Ordinance 26-008 passed unanimously.

Significant debate occurred over striking language requiring members to justify remote attendance based on health, safety, or welfare concerns. Trustees argued chairs should not have to determine if someone's excuse is 'good enough.' Trustee Beck moved to amend striking sentences requiring justification; Trustee Clark seconded. The amendment passed with Trustees Hermling and Clark voting no. The original motion as amended then passed with the same two dissenting votes.

Board discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market relocated to Kennedy Park after losing their previous location to development. Concerns raised included parking on grass creating safety issues and potential field damage. Renee explained the market grew from 25 to 53 vendor stalls and vendors appreciate having vehicles nearby. Board acknowledged Mont Miller Park was considered but rejected as too far from traffic. Trustee Beck questioned costs but was assured the market operates at no loss. Discussion only - no action taken.

Board approved a secondhand article and jewelry dealer license for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street. Chief Hunt explained the licensing provides safety checks for stolen merchandise through a police-accessible database. Motion was amended to require satisfactory background check completion and occupancy permit before license is issued. Passed unanimously as amended.

President appointed Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lopes, passed unanimously. Parks, HR/Finance, and CLIPS still have openings. Board requested that committee applicant resumes be included in future packets.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52nd Street spoke during public comment criticizing the hiring of outside engineering consultants at $108,000 when an engineering technician position remains unfilled. He questioned why Lisa Beck was introduced as a Safer board member before being officially appointed and criticized lack of transparency around committee appointments. He also complained about missing information on Kennedy Park funding in the meeting packet and called for better administrator accountability.
actionItems: Ordinance 26-010 increasing park and recreation impact fees approved unanimously
Resolution 2026-015 amending village fee schedule approved unanimously
Ordinance 26-008 on mobile food vendor licensing approved unanimously
Ordinance 26-009 amending standing committees approved unanimously
Ordinance 26-011 on nuisances approved unanimously
Ordinance 26-012 on bicycle and e-bike regulations approved unanimously
Resolution 2026-014 supporting sustainable transportation funding approved unanimously
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove justification requirements - passed with two dissenting votes
Golden Mua and Stephanie Daniels appointed to CLIPS committee
Secondhand dealer license for Scoffield Coin and Hobby approved contingent on background check and occupancy permit
Comprehensive Plan steering committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 PM
Staff to include committee applicant resumes in future board packets
Weston Avenue speed limit study to return to board after construction completion

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board held its adjourned organizational meeting, approving committee appointments and electing Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to at-large executive committee positions. The board unanimously approved a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster-related debris following the April tornado and flooding that devastated parts of Ringle and other communities, with fees reduced from $65/ton to approximately $36.94/ton for affected residents.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Reading of meeting notice
Roll call
Committee appointments discussion and vote
Election of at-large executive committee members
Consent agenda items C10-C14A
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 25-26: Social services budget amendment
Resolution 26-26: Landfill tipping fee modification for disaster relief
Announcements and adjournment

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, emphasizing goals of increasing perspective diversity, developing new supervisors as leaders, and honoring supervisor preferences. He noted 34% of the board is in its first or second term. The motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims, was carried but not unanimously. Supervisor Nisa noted he was marked absent but did not receive the option to vote, indicating he was an 'I' on the motion.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg spoke in support of Supervisor Leur, citing her advocacy for the county's youngest and oldest residents. Paper ballots were distributed and supervisors selected two candidates. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the executive committee.
Items C10 through C14A were approved on motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously by voice vote.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of Home Placement Reserve Increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The resolution was carried but not unanimously, with no discussion recorded.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The resolution was carried but not unanimously, with no discussion recorded.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Landfill Tipping Fee Modification for Disaster Relief
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard and Director Hagen Busher presented this resolution from the Solid Waste Management Board in response to the April tornado and flooding. The proposal reduces tipping fees from $65/ton to approximately $36.94/ton for certified disaster debris, with the state waiving $13.10 and the Town of Ringle waiving $2.33. The board suspended rules unanimously to consider the resolution since it bypassed standing committees. Public speakers Jessica Tolesie and Matt Haybeck requested the county waive fees entirely for affected residents, noting many are underinsured and waiting on insurance adjusters. Supervisor Gao questioned the fee calculations and Supervisor Rosenberg requested continued discussion by the Solid Waste Board. The resolution was approved unanimously on motion by Supervisor Arstead, seconded by Supervisor Morash.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special dispensation to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Tolesie advocated for Marathon County to completely waive fees, noting the state, DNR, and Town of Ringle have all waived their portions, and that local businesses have donated up to $40,000 in cleanup efforts. She requested a six-month fee waiver. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of homeowners whose houses must be demolished, noting 44 homes are affected and some insurance adjusters haven't arrived yet, requesting more time for fee relief.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to at-large executive committee positions
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees to $36.94/ton for certified disaster debris through June 17, 2025
Administrator Leonard to continue discussions with Community Foundation regarding reimbursement mechanism for uninsured debris disposal costs
Solid Waste Management Board directed to develop long-term policy on disaster-related fee reductions within six months
Vice chairs directed to shepherd strategic plan updates within their committees

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board that will only waive reconnect fees for septic systems on properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public comment
Approval of March 31st, 2026 committee minutes
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Urban Residential/General Agriculture to Rural Residential, Town of Green Valley)
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture, Town of McMillan)
Consideration of fee waivers for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation: Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation: Conservation, Planning, and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried with no opposition.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Town of Green Valley)
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from Urban Residential and General Agriculture to Rural Residential for a proposed land division. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026, and staff recommended approval based on consistency with the comprehensive plan. No public comment was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and the town's approval, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidz rezoning petition (Town of McMillan)
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Lever asked about the conservancy designation; Pogle explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, and the rezone will make these conforming uses. He confirmed no natural areas would be lost. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waivers for April 17, 2026 severe weather damage
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for properties damaged by the tornado affecting the towns of Ringle, Eastston, and villages of Cronin Weter and Weston. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concern about fairness to individual disaster victims not in declared areas. Marilyn Bend asked about septic hookup costs. After discussion, Supervisor Drebeck moved to amend the resolution to only waive reconnect fees ($160) for septic systems, not other zoning or septic fees. Supervisor Kurt seconded the amendment. The amendment carried. The amended resolution was then approved to forward to county board, though not unanimously.

Director David Hagenbusher presented an overview of the department's operations, including the landfill facility operating since 1980, the renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy generating royalties for the county, and upcoming capital projects including $6 million in cell construction. He highlighted the leachate treatment challenge requiring on-site treatment by 2032 due to PFAS regulations, with current hauling costs of $90,000 in April alone. An environmental loan application for treatment facilities will be resubmitted for 2027 funding.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department overview
discussions.body: Tom Lovelin, County Forest Administrator, presented on behalf of Director Jamie who had an emergency. He explained the department serves both city and county with 44 full-time and 120 seasonal staff, managing 17 county parks, 30,700 acres of county forest, and 37 city parks. A Nine Mile County Forest study showed out-of-town visitors generate $3.25 million for the local economy. Supervisor Sefeld announced Tom is retiring June 1st after nearly 30 years, with Joe Tucker selected as the new county forest administrator.

actionItems: Rezoning from Urban Residential/General Agriculture to Rural Residential in Town of Green Valley approved and forwarded to county board
Rezoning from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture in Town of McMillan approved and forwarded to county board
Modified fee waiver resolution (reconnect fees only) for tornado-damaged properties forwarded to county board for consideration
CPZ staff to track costs and requests related to tornado fee waivers for future policy development
Solid Waste to resubmit environmental loan application for leachate treatment facility by October 2026 for 2027 funding

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center until next month due to the chair's absence, approved installation of new kayak launch signs along the Plover River, and received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising and trail maintenance. Public comment included opposition to dogs at the pool and criticism of the Kennedy Park funding transparency.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Approval of minutes from April 27, 2026 meeting
Public comments
Kennedy Park update
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center
Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail
Yellow Banks kayak launch signs
Topics for future meetings and staff remarks

discussions.body: Minutes from the April 27, 2026 meeting were approved with a motion and second. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

Staff presented a timeline document showing the project history from the comprehensive plan approval in November 2023 through the May 9th ribbon cutting. Director Osterbrink explained the village had plans to contribute approximately $7 million of the $14 million total project for infrastructure like bathrooms and shelters built in the 1970s. Scott questioned the fundraising timeline, and Osterbrink clarified the agreement with Friends of Kennedy Park references a 10-year timeframe for project completion. The Friends group plans to announce fundraising totals at their June tournament. They currently have approximately $84-85,000 at the community foundation toward a $117,000 goal for field lights.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff presented photos and maps showing tree removal locations and mat installation over gas pipelines. Dan explained the mats are 8x8 wood construction mats used to protect infrastructure during equipment access. The mats are now village property with extras available for other trail projects.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The item was deferred to next month because Katrina, who requested the discussion, was absent due to an unforeseen issue. The presiding officer noted resident Andy's objection would be documented but wanted to ensure Katrina had opportunity to speak on the matter.

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail
discussions.body: Staff explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete and memorial benches, noting the pressure washer cannot access the trail due to bridge width limitations and steep ditches. Options discussed included anti-vandalism coatings, sealer applications, and potential volunteer work days. Committee member Melissa suggested community members would likely volunteer to help. No formal action was taken; staff will investigate cleaning options.

Dan Hager from Marathon County Parks (2625 Northwestern Avenue) presented version 7 of proposed signage for Plover River kayak launches. Three signs will be installed in Village of Weston at Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks locations. Signs include QR codes for river gauge information and donation links to Marathon County Parks Foundation. The Highway J launch will likely be closed this season due to bridge replacement. Motion to approve sign installation passed unanimously.

Staff mentioned receiving an email requesting participation in placing food allergy awareness signs on playgrounds. Signs would be provided free and encourage keeping food at picnic tables rather than play equipment. Committee agreed to add this as a future discussion topic.

publicComment: Two speakers provided public comment. Andy (6504 [street inaudible]) spoke against allowing dogs at the public pool, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' Jim Pensan (5002 [street] Weston), speaking via Zoom under the name 'Vern M', criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation and questioned the Kennedy Park funding report, calling the $8,700 expenditure figure 'too coincidental' and asking where the $10,000 grant money and glow event proceeds are reflected. He characterized the Friends of Kennedy Park financial reporting as 'extremely convoluted and misleading.'
actionItems: Dog Day at aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting
Staff to investigate graffiti removal options and anti-vandalism coatings for trail amenities
Kayak launch signs approved for installation at Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks locations
Food allergy awareness signage discussion added to future agenda
Mock Miller Park master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for next Tuesday
Bike and pedestrian plan open house scheduled for June 11th, 5-7 p.m. in training room
Next Parks Committee meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged budget status reports. The meeting featured an extensive educational presentation by Municipal Clerk Pam on clerk office operations, elections, licensing, and tax collection. Committee member Evan announced his resignation as he is moving away from the area.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Public comments
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
April budget status report
TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports for April
Legal details report for April
Educational presentations - Municipal Clerk's office budget and operations
Old business, new business, staff remarks
Adjournment

discussions.item: Approval of minutes from previous meeting
discussions.body: Motion to approve made by Ernling, seconded by Daniels. Passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: April budget status report and TIFF reports
discussions.body: Committee reviewed budget status reports. Jessica confirmed that TIFF bond payoff is anticipated for October/November, with bonds due October 1st. The CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds. Jessica confirmed the numbers look good and they will be able to pay off the debt. Motion to acknowledge items 5, 6, and 7 made by Daniels, seconded by Soaper. Motion carried unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented comprehensive overview of clerk operations including elections, licensing, tax collection, and pet licensing. She noted the office went from four full-time staff to two-and-a-half, with her and Sarah handling the entire 2024 presidential election alone. Discussion covered election postage costs increasing from $6,000 to $15,000 for presidential election years, chief inspector pay potentially increasing from current rate to $15/hour, and pet licensing fee increase from $10 to $20 due to Marathon County requirements. Pam noted 84% of tax collection ($22 million) is handled at the clerk's office counter. Committee discussed the $30,000 annual cost of the village newsletter 'The Wire' and past attempts to reduce or eliminate mailing.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market has been going well, with her present for 6-7 hours each Saturday. The market is full with food trucks, coffee vendors, craft people, and greenhouse vendors. Produce vendors expected to increase mid to late June. Committee member noted vendors expressed satisfaction with the new setup and location. Some discussion of parking concerns on Alderson and Newport streets, with staff confirming east side of Alderson is signed no parking while west side now allows parking.

Evan announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though staying in the metro area. He thanked the committee for the opportunity to serve. The chair noted there are now multiple open spots on this and other committees and encouraged outreach to interested citizens.

actionItems: CDA resolution needed to call TIFF bonds, anticipated this summer
Clerk considering increasing chief inspector pay to $15/hour for next election term
Seeking additional part-time clerk staff member
Recruit citizens for open committee positions
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee approved amendments to nuisance ordinances, recommended an e-bike ordinance with restrictions on e-motos, and authorized demolition of an abandoned structure at 4104 Shore Avenue. The committee also approved fire inspection requirements for mobile food vendors and discussed but deferred action on overnight parking regulations.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Public comments
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Department reports - Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police
Proposed amendments to Chapter 50 Nuisances
E-bicycle ordinance amendments in Chapter 82
Overnight parking regulations discussion
Crosswalk request on Alderson Street
Mobile food vendors fire inspection amendments
Address corrections for E911 compliance
Raze request for 4104 Shore Avenue

discussions.body: Jim Senalt spoke regarding concerns about the new farmers market location at Kennedy Park, stating his opinion that grass areas will be destroyed by vehicles parking on grass. He questioned whether current village code prohibits parking on grass and raised concerns about ADA-compliant parking spaces being placed on grass rather than asphalt. He also questioned the new fire inspection requirement for mobile food vendors and noted that some code enforcement cases have been pending for years without visible progress.

Minutes from the previous meeting were approved unanimously on motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka. The committee acknowledged reports from Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS (Safer), and Police Department. Roman Olsen noted that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement roll after the property sold at auction and the new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt reported the police department had 2,394 calls in March (77 per day) and has hired its 40th officer. Reports were acknowledged unanimously on motion by Chwanka, seconded by Olsen.

The committee reviewed final amendments to nuisance ordinances, including switching tree limb height requirements to 14 and 10 feet over streets and walkways. The motion to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees passed unanimously, with motion by Olsen and second by Compi.

Extensive discussion occurred about regulating e-bikes, e-motos, and electric scooters. Chief Hunt strongly recommended prohibiting e-motos from roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Committee member Mike Compi raised concerns about electric scooters used by middle school students. The committee agreed to add language prohibiting e-motos from roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, passed unanimously to recommend approval with the e-moto prohibition added.

Brent Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour overnight parking restriction (4 AM to 4 PM, October 31-May 1) is significantly different from surrounding municipalities which typically have 5-hour windows. Michael Wodalski from Public Works suggested potentially eliminating the overnight restriction and instead signing specific problem areas. Chief Hunt expressed preference for keeping overnight parking restrictions to help clear roads before plowing. The committee deferred action, directing Michael Wodalski to identify specific problem areas and bring recommendations back in July or August.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between their property and DC Everest school parking lot. Michael Wodalski estimated a basic crosswalk would cost approximately $2,000, while a lighted system would cost $25,000-$30,000. The committee discussed having Mount Olive cover the costs since it primarily benefits their congregation. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, passed unanimously to recommend board approval contingent on Mount Olive agreeing to cover installation costs.

Chief Hunt explained a new regional fire inspection program where food trucks inspected by any participating department (Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, or Weston) receive a sticker valid for all jurisdictions. He noted Wausau opted out to maintain their own inspection program. Fire Marshal Marty has already conducted inspections, with one vendor failing due to improper LP piping and unsecured propane containers. Motion by Chwanka, seconded by Compi, passed unanimously to approve Ordinance 26-8.

Staff presented three address corrections needed for E911 compliance: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing separate addresses), 3913/3917 Ross Avenue (out of sequence addresses), and 2211 Radkey Avenue (new duplex on private drive). Chief Hunt noted an additional addressing issue at Weston Villa Apartments where eight apartments have separate addresses. Motion passed unanimously to recommend board approval.

Staff requested authorization to begin raze proceedings on an abandoned structure at 4104 Shore Avenue. The property has been abandoned since 2010-2011 and was previously considered for a fire training burn but deemed too unsafe. Staff noted the current owners had housed chickens in the structure and have agreed to the demolition. Costs will be placed on the tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Compi, passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding the new farmers market location at Kennedy Park, expressing concerns about vehicles parking on grass destroying the park, ADA parking compliance issues with handicap spaces on grass rather than asphalt, questioning the origin of new fire inspection requirements for food trucks, and noting that some code enforcement cases have been pending for years without visible progress.
actionItems: Chapter 50 Nuisances amendments recommended for Board of Trustees approval
E-bike ordinance with e-moto prohibition recommended for Board of Trustees approval
Michael Wodalski to prepare list of specific problem areas for overnight parking restrictions for July/August meeting
Michael Wodalski to contact Mount Olive Church regarding covering crosswalk installation costs
Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections approved
Address corrections for three properties recommended for Board approval
Raze order for 4104 Shore Avenue authorized to proceed
Staff to investigate property condition at Sternberg and South Timber intersection
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 7, 2025 at 5:00 PM

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Call to Order
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Preliminary Discussion Regarding Potential Litigation 19.85 (g)
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate
Adjourn

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Call to Order
Roll Call
Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Reading of the Mission Statement
Excellence in Action: Maine Elementary
Public and Student Comment
Approve Consent Agenda (Action Requested)
Old/Recurring Business
New Business
Open Forum
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Adjourn

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Arbor Day Proclamation
Week of the Young Child Proclamation
National Preservation Month Proclamation
Workers Memorial Day Proclamation
March 24, 2026 Regular Common Council Minutes
April 14, 2026 Regular Common Council Minutes
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
